evidence: c2c_service.go exposes a `C2CService` interface with a generic `Execute(action actions.Action, ssid, password string) error` method while action parsing/dispatch is also defined in the same package via a local `c2cActionDispatcher`.

`ProcessC2CMessage` accepts any `C2CService` from `serviceFactory` and directly calls `service.Execute`, so consumers can bypass the shared action contract path (`ParseC2CAction` / `NewActionDispatcher`) by providing an alternate implementation.

The protocol contract is centralized in actions.go (`ParseAction`, `actionMetasForNamespace`, `SupportedC2CActions`), but `system/wifi/service` still requires each caller to trust injected implementations to honor that contract.

Multiple files define large runtime seam structs with many pass-through function fields (for example `RuntimeFns` in `goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_seams.go` and `WireguardRuntime` in `goseg/docker/orchestration/container/wireguard.go`).

`Startup`/background orchestration paths compose several nested op interfaces whose default implementations mostly delegate to package-level functions (`goseg/startup_orchestrator.go`, `goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go`, `goseg/importer/importer.go`).

Artifact/config write paths contain an additional wrapper layer where `writeWGConfigArtifact` builds an options struct and immediately delegates to `writeConfigArtifact`, which itself mostly forwards into `artifactwriter.Write` (`goseg/docker/orchestration/container/wireguard.go`, `goseg/docker/orchestration/container/helpers.go`, `goseg/docker/orchestration/internal/artifactwriter/artifact_writer.go`).
`C2CServiceExecutor` stores `connectToWiFi` and `restartGroundSeg` as function fields and calls them directly with no nil guards.

`ProcessC2CMessage` validates only the factory and service object, but does not validate function-level invariants before dispatch, so miswired adapters can panic at runtime instead of returning deterministic errors.
The README-level governance for this batch emphasizes contract-boundary quality gates, but this file-level critical path has no local precondition tests for constructor/dispatcher wiring failures.
`goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_seams.go` defines `NewRuntime(fns ...RuntimeFns)` but only applies `fns[0]`, silently discarding all additional seam function-set arguments.
`goseg/docker/lifecycle/lifecycle.go` uses the same variadic pattern but correctly iterates every option, establishing inconsistent constructor semantics across adjacent seam/lifecycle abstractions.

`goseg/config/config_update.go` models map mutations as additive-only builders (`WithAuthorizedSession`, `WithUnauthorizedSession`, `WithDiskWarning`) and applies them only when patch map length is > 0, creating an unclear path for explicit map clearing/removal semantics.

`goseg/system/wifi/service/c2c_service.go` executes injected runtime callbacks (`connectToWiFi`, `restartGroundSeg`) without nil checks, so a partially constructed `C2CServiceExecutor` can panic at call time instead of returning a typed error.

`goseg/system/wifi/service/c2c_service.go` and `goseg/internal/seams/runtime.go` show two different seam strategies: one suite returns explicit fallback errors for missing hooks, while another allows nil callback dereference.

`goseg/shipworkflow/urbit_operations.go` frequently wraps upstream errors with `%v` (for example around lines with `Failed to get ship status...` and many MinIO/alias/rollback paths), which drops unwrapability for callers that depend on typed causes.

`goseg/system/disk.go` similarly formats many upstream failures with `%v` (for example `Failed to lsblk`, `Failed to create ext4 filesystem`, `failed to write to fstab`) instead of `%w`, resulting in inconsistent observability and retry/classification behavior versus other call paths that do preserve error causes.
error_consistency.issues_preventing_higher_score: Error wrapping strategy is inconsistent at module boundaries: some high-value paths preserve underlying causes (`%w`/joined errors) while adjacent paths in the same subsystem erase them with `%v` formatting, which weakens callers’ ability to classify and aggregate failures.

findings.summary: Protocol action contracts are duplicated across layers instead of owned by a single boundary.
evidence: `ActionC2CConnect` is defined in `goseg/protocol/actions/actions.go` as the canonical protocol token.
`goseg/system/wifi/service/c2c_service.go` introduces `ConnectAction` as a second alias assigned to the same token.
Both identifiers represent the same semantic command, so any rename or semantic change must be mirrored manually in multiple modules.
findings.suggestion: Delete the local `ConnectAction` alias and use the protocol contract symbol directly at all call sites, or introduce a single exported canonical symbol in `actions` and remove per-layer command aliases so contract changes are made once.

findings.summary: C2C action dispatch is bound at package scope, increasing coupling and reducing composability across module boundaries.
evidence: `goseg/system/wifi/service/c2c_service.go` defines `c2cActionDispatcher` as a global variable initialized with `actions.NewActionDispatcher(...)`.
All command execution paths read from this singleton dispatcher, which ties this package’s runtime behavior to import-time construction of bindings and fixed semantics.
findings.suggestion: Refactor to construct the dispatcher explicitly (for example in `NewC2CServiceForAdapter`/`ProcessC2CMessage`) and pass it in as a dependency so action wiring is explicit, testable, and replaceable by callers without editing global package state.

findings.summary: Runtime seam abstractions are over-bagged and mostly delegate straight through to concrete functions.
evidence: `RuntimeFns` in `goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_seams.go` exposes many one-function fields and constructor logic only re-binds them to defaults with minimal policy.

`startup_orchestrator.go` splits startup work into several callback interfaces (`startBackgroundServicesOps`, `startBackgroundStartramOps`, `startBackgroundOptionalOps`) with defaults that immediately call package functions.
`operations_runtime.go` defines `uploadImportOps`, `aliasLookupOps`, `cleanDeleteOps` as mostly injectable function containers with wrapper defaults and no additional translation.
`importer.go` defines `importerRuntime` with several function fields where default runtime delegates directly to concrete functions, creating another thin seam layer.
findings.suggestion: Refactor these into narrower domain-specific interfaces/adapters (e.g., `ContainerRuntime`, `WorkflowRuntime`, `ConfigRuntime`) and pass concrete adapters directly instead of broad per-function bags. Remove duplicate interface layers when a single underlying function set already exists, and keep seam interfaces at true test seams, not for every leaf operation.

findings.summary: Wireguard config artifact path stacks multiple options structs and copy-through wrappers with no meaningful behavior.
evidence: `writeWGConfigArtifact` in `goseg/docker/orchestration/container/wireguard.go` builds a `writeConfigArtifactOptions` struct and forwards directly to `writeConfigArtifact` without adding domain logic.
`writeConfigArtifact` in `goseg/docker/orchestration/container/helpers.go` only normalizes one field and forwards into `artifactwriter.Write`.

`artifactwriter.Write` then performs default fallbacks and orchestration of copy vs write; the container-level helper adds another near-identical struct and call boundary before reaching same behavior.
findings.suggestion: Consolidate these into a single `ArtifactWriter` abstraction with one concrete configuration object per domain (e.g., `WriteWireguardConfigSpec`) and remove the redundant container helper/options layer; make `writeWGConfigArtifact` either perform a domain-specific policy decision or delete it and call the writer directly.

findings.summary: Wi‑Fi C2C command contract is not sealed: injected services can execute raw actions without the shared protocol dispatcher contract.
evidence: `c2c_service.go` defines `C2CService` with `Execute(action actions.Action, ssid, password string) error` and `ProcessC2CMessage` invokes that method on any provided service.
The same file also defines `c2cActionDispatcher` and action-binding logic, so contract enforcement exists but is not guaranteed by interface shape.
This creates two execution paths for C2C actions depending on implementation choice, weakening boundary integrity between protocol contracts and runtime service behavior.
findings.suggestion: Refactor the interface so `Execute` is not publicly substitutable: keep `C2CService` focused on domain operations (`ConnectToWiFi`, `RestartGroundSeg`) and move action dispatch into a package-private function that always goes through the action contract parser/dispatcher. Add an explicit adapter-level test that proves every supported action routes through `actions.NewActionDispatcher`.

findings.summary: Critical Wi‑Fi adapter service constructor path has unguarded runtime panic potential that is not covered by local contract tests.
evidence: `NewC2CServiceForAdapter` accepts `connectToWiFi` and `restartGroundSeg` function arguments without validation.
`ConnectToWiFi` and `RestartGroundSeg` dereference those function fields directly (`s.connectToWiFi(...)`, `s.restartGroundSeg(...)`) with no nil guard.

Governance docs in `README.md` call out explicit runtime boundary and quality expectations for contract-bearing paths, but this code path has no local guardrails and would fail via panic rather than deterministic error.
Existing expectations imply adapter boundary behavior should be robustly tested for contract miswiring, especially in command execution entry points.
findings.suggestion: Add precondition checks in `NewC2CServiceForAdapter` (or the methods) that return a clear error when dependencies are nil, and add tests for miswired adapter dependencies to lock behavior (error propagation instead of panic).

findings.summary: Runtime seam constructor silently ignores all but the first variadic seam config, making `NewRuntime` behavior lossy and surprising.
evidence: `NewRuntime(fns ...RuntimeFns)` initializes `cfg := RuntimeFns{}` and then only assigns `cfg = fns[0]` when `len(fns) > 0`; no loop merges later values.
The corresponding option-style constructor in `goseg/docker/lifecycle/lifecycle.go` iterates through variadic options, so callers cannot infer consistent API intent across packages.
Current tests do not cover passing multiple `RuntimeFns` values, so this silent drop is unguarded.
findings.suggestion: Change `NewRuntime` to merge all provided `RuntimeFns` values deterministically (e.g., start with zero-value struct and apply each via explicit merge/overlay), or change signature to accept a single config struct if only one is intended; add a test asserting that passing multiple values applies all provided overrides.

findings.summary: Configuration patch model conflates additive updates with replacement/clear semantics for map fields.
evidence: `hasUpdates` returns true for map sections only when their lengths are greater than zero, so an empty map cannot represent an explicit clear operation.
`applyConfPatch` applies map writes under the same non-empty gate, meaning callers cannot express intent to clear `AuthorizedSessions`, `UnauthorizedSessions`, or `DiskWarnings` via patch.
Patch builder helpers only support `With*Session` and `WithDiskWarning` addition-style methods, with no explicit clear/remove API for map entries.
findings.suggestion: Introduce explicit patch operations for map fields (e.g., `ClearAuthorizedSessions`, `RemoveUnauthorizedSession`, `SetAuthorizedSessions(map[string].., mode=replace)`), or encode maps as pointer/tri-state values so empty vs nil is distinguishable and clear semantics are represented explicitly.

findings.summary: C2C executor can panic on nil callbacks instead of returning a deterministic error.
evidence: `goseg/system/wifi/service/c2c_service.go` constructors accept arbitrary functions for `connectToWiFi` and `restartGroundSeg` but do not validate them.
`ConnectToWiFi` and `RestartGroundSeg` unconditionally invoke function fields, so nil fields cause a nil-function panic rather than an error return.

Other seam constructors in this codebase commonly substitute fallback behavior or return explicit configuration errors when callbacks are missing (`goseg/internal/seams/runtime.go`), so this is a strategy mismatch at a boundary.
findings.suggestion: Add constructor-time validation (or runtime nil checks in each method) that returns a concrete error when `connectToWiFi` or `restartGroundSeg` are unset, and expose that failure through `ProcessC2CMessage` rather than panicking.

findings.summary: Critical ship workflow wrappers drop underlying errors with `%v`, breaking consistent error-chain semantics.
evidence: In `goseg/shipworkflow/urbit_operations.go`, many return sites use `fmt.Errorf("...: %v", err)` in long-running operation paths (for example around pack/meld/chop/alias/container operations).
Nearby return sites in the same package do use `%w`, so behavior is mixed within one boundary depending on operation path.
Inconsistent wrapping prevents callers and tests from relying on `errors.Is`/`errors.As` at orchestration boundaries even where they expect wrapped causality.
findings.suggestion: Normalize `shipworkflow` return conventions by wrapping all forwarded errors with `%w`, and keep `%v` only for message-only formatting when no underlying error should be propagated.

findings.summary: Disk setup and fs-operations lose error causality, reducing diagnosability consistency.
evidence: `goseg/system/disk.go` returns upstream command/system errors with `%v` in multiple IO and filesystem paths (`ListHardDisks`, `CreateGroundSegFilesystem`, `SetupTmpDir` helpers), so the original error type is not preserved.
`goseg/system/wifi.go` commonly uses wrapped returns (`%w`) for command and state transitions, so sibling system package boundaries behave differently under failure.
The mixed approach makes downstream consumers harder to reason about because some failures can be typed/inspected while adjacent failures become opaque strings.
findings.suggestion: Adopt a package-wide wrapping rule in `system/*` (e.g., wrap lower-level errors with `%w` for all propagation paths, and only mask when intentionally redacting), then add tests asserting `errors.Is` works for representative disk-wiring failures.

findings.summary: Config read methods use write locks, forcing global serialization across read-heavy handlers.
evidence: `Conf()` takes `confMutex.Lock()` and `defer confMutex.Unlock()` even for immutable read access.
`UrbitConf()` in `goseg/config/urbit.go` also takes `urbitMutex.Lock()` for read-only lookup.
Handler paths call these accessors frequently (`handler/authsvc`, `handler/devsvc`) while holding other orchestration/runtime locks in normal operation.
findings.suggestion: Switch these read-mostly paths to `sync.RWMutex` and use `RLock/RUnlock` for reads, then reserve write locks for mutation paths. Consider adding a `ConfigSnapshot()` API that returns immutable snapshots for read-heavy flows.

findings.summary: Config map patch parsing rejects valid JSON values due to strict `int`/typed-map assertions.
evidence: `buildConfigPatch` asserts `swapVal`, `penpaiCores`, `c2cInterval` as `int` and returns type errors for `float64` values.
`authorizedSessions` and `diskWarning` are asserted as concrete maps instead of decoding from decoded JSON shape.
The same module also accepts `piers` only as `[]interface{}` or `[]string`, indicating mixed contract handling.
findings.suggestion: Accept JSON payloads using a typed DTO with `json.Decoder.UseNumber()` and normalize numeric fields explicitly (`int(num)`) and nested map shapes through helper decoders instead of direct assertions.

findings.summary: Broadcast loop discards websocket broadcast failures, masking operational regressions.
evidence: `runBroadcastTickWithRuntime` calls `rt.broadcastToClientsFn()` and does not inspect the returned error.
No recovery/backoff/health signal is emitted when broadcast fanout fails.
Tests in `broadcast/loop_test.go` verify only successful broadcast callbacks.
findings.suggestion: Capture and act on the error from `broadcastToClientsFn`: log with context, increment a metrics counter, and optionally trigger a retry/backoff policy or degraded-mode transition.

findings.summary: Session key path initialization uses a local shadow variable, leaving global path state stale.
evidence: `initializeConfig` defines `keyPath := filepath.Join(BasePath, "settings", "session.key")` inside the empty-key path branch.
`UpdateConfTyped(WithKeyfile(keyPath))` updates config but does not update package-level `keyPath` used elsewhere as canonical path state.
Subsequent code depends on `config`-level state for auth/session key behavior, so dual-path state can diverge.
findings.suggestion: Use package-level `keyPath` consistently in bootstrap/update flow (assign to shared var, then persist), or remove global `keyPath` in favor of deriving from config snapshot everywhere.

findings.summary: Exporter walk callback defers `Close` inside a tight loop, deferring all file descriptors until handler exit.
evidence: In `ExportHandler`, each `walkForExporter` callback does `fileReader, err := os.Open(path)` followed by `defer fileReader.Close()`.
This defer executes when `ExportHandler` returns, not per file iteration, so descriptor count grows with every exported file.
Current tests only cover a small one-file success path and token checks, not many-file stress behavior.
findings.suggestion: Refactor to `if _, err := ...; err != nil { ... }` then `if _, err := io.Copy(...); err != nil { ... }` with explicit `fileReader.Close()`/`file.Close()` before callback returns.

findings.summary: Failure-mode tests are missing for high-impact runtime loops and export traversal behavior.
evidence: Broadcast loop tests validate happy-path tick and nil-error callback behavior; no test covers `broadcastToClientsFn` returning an error.
Exporter tests do not include multi-file exports, induced file-open errors during walk, or descriptor-limit stress cases tied to per-iteration `defer` behavior.
This leaves two production error paths behaviorally unverified in automated tests.
findings.suggestion: Add focused tests for `broadcastToClientsFn` error propagation and a synthetic multi-file export that forces per-file open/read errors, asserting cleanup, error response code, and absence of resource leaks.
